name: Jamming User Interfaces
short_description: A technique using programmable particle jamming to make interface surfaces switch between soft and rigid on command, with high-resolution shape sensing for malleable, shape-changing devices.
long_description: Jamming User Interfaces, presented at UIST 2012 by Sean Follmer, Daniel Leithinger, Alex Olwal, Nadia Cheng, and Hiroshi Ishii, applies particle jamming, a phenomenon where granular material packed in a flexible membrane stiffens under vacuum, to create surfaces whose stiffness is programmatically controlled, enabling tunable haptic feedback, deformation, and adjustable affordances. The authors built a compact, low-power pneumatic system for mobile devices and a fast, silent hydraulic system, and added high-resolution shape sensing using index-matched particles and fluids with optical capture as well as capacitive and electric-field sensing. As physical AI and interaction design, it matters because it lets a single physical surface dynamically reconfigure its material properties, blurring the line between input device, display, and tangible interface. It is a foundational reference in the shape-changing and malleable interface literature.
ai_note: The system is a material, actuation, and sensing technique (particle jamming with pneumatic/hydraulic control and optical/capacitive shape sensing); no AI/ML is involved.

images.caption: Jamming User Interfaces: a malleable particle-jamming surface that switches between soft and rigid states for tablets and mobile devices.
images.alt_text: A flexible interface surface using particle jamming, shown deforming and stiffening, demonstrating programmable shape and stiffness on a handheld device.

interactions.title: Sculpt a form, then freeze it
interactions.description: With the Tunable Clay prototype you press and mold a soft, granular surface freely by hand; pull a vacuum and the material instantly locks rigid, holding the exact shape you just sculpted so it can be inspected or used as a control.
interactions.source_label: MIT Tangible Media Group — project page

interactions.title: Buttons that rise and vanish under the screen
interactions.description: The Behind-the-Tablet prototype puts a jamming layer beneath a touch surface so the device can stiffen selected regions into physical buttons or ridges when you need them, then soften back to a flat slate when you do not.

interactions.title: A see-through pad that pushes back
interactions.description: The Transparent Haptic Lens uses index-matched particles and fluid so the jamming cell stays optically clear over a display, letting you feel programmable tactile resistance on top of an image you can still see through.

interactions.title: Bend a phone, and it stays bent
interactions.description: The ShapePhone prototype is a handheld device with a compact, low-power pneumatic jamming core, so you can flex it into a curved or folded posture and have it hold that form as a saved physical configuration.
interactions.source_label: MIT Tangible Media Group — project page

interactions.title: The surface reads the shape you made
interactions.description: Because the jamming cells double as sensors — via optical capture of index-matched particles or capacitive and electric-field sensing — the system captures the high-resolution deformation you impose, so manipulating the material is itself an input.

interactions.title: Dial the stiffness of a stylus or knob
interactions.description: Programmable particle stiffness lets a tool change how hard it pushes back: a jamming-based grip or slider can feel loose and pliable for coarse moves, then firm up to give precise, detented resistance for fine control.

interactions.title: A rehab grip that stiffens to match the patient
interactions.description: A handheld jamming cell could soften for a weak grasp and progressively firm up as a patient regains strength, turning the same object into a tunable resistance device a therapist sets per session — grounded in the technique's programmable, per-region stiffness control.

interactions.title: Capture a clay maquette straight into CAD
interactions.description: Sculpt a quick form on the Tunable Clay surface, freeze it, and let the built-in shape sensing digitize the deformation as a 3D mesh — using the same optical/capacitive sensing the prototypes already demonstrate to turn hand-molded shapes into editable models.

sources.title: Jamming user interfaces: programmable particle stiffness and sensing for malleable and shape-changing devices
Jamming User Interfaces
sources.publisher: MIT Media Lab Tangible Media Group
